lesson.title: The Mice Who Lived in a Shoe
lesson.essentialQuestion: How do characters work together to solve a problem?
lesson.objective: Students will read and discuss the story, use vocabulary in context, practice target phonics patterns, identify common and proper nouns, and answer cause-and-effect comprehension questions.
lesson.writingConnection: Opinion writing: state an opinion, give reasons, revise, edit, and publish.
standards: 2.RL.1 Ask and answer questions to demonstrate understanding of a text.
2.RL.3 Describe how characters respond to major events and challenges.
2.RF.3 Know and apply grade-level phonics and word analysis skills.
2.L.1 Demonstrate command of conventions of standard English grammar and usage.
wida: ELP Standard 1: Social and Instructional Language
ELP Standard 2: Language for Language Arts
beforeReading: Use pictures or gestures for merchant, timber, platform, and shelter.
Review phonics patterns before students encounter them in connected text.
Ask students to predict how the characters might solve a problem.
duringReading: Ask: What happened? Why did it happen?
Use partner talk before whole-group responses.
afterReading: Connect the story to opinion writing.
teachLikeMrsParrish: Do not skip vocabulary before reading. It makes the story more accessible.
Spend extra time on merchant because most students do not use that word naturally.
Use partner talk before asking students to answer cause-and-effect questions.
The Open Court packet is worth using because it reinforces what the assessment expects.
This is a good lesson for modeling how characters respond when plans change.

planbook.objective: Students will read and discuss The Mice Who Lived in a Shoe while practicing target phonics, vocabulary, grammar, and comprehension skills.
planbook.lessonInstruction: I Do: Model vocabulary, phonics patterns, and cause/effect thinking. We Do: Read and discuss the text together using partner talk. You Do: Complete skills practice and respond to comprehension questions.
planbook.differentiation: Use visuals, oral rehearsal, sentence frames, partner support, small-group reteach, and reduced response options as needed.
planbook.homeworkEvidence: Skills practice, oral responses, comprehension questions, vocabulary use, and teacher observation.
planbook.materialsResources: Open Court Teacher Edition, skills practice packet, vocabulary cards, phonics assessment, vocabulary assessment, comprehension assessment, GUM assessment, pencils, student notebooks.
planbook.notesReflection: Record what worked, what needed reteaching, and what a co-teacher should know before teaching this lesson.

intervention: Use UFLI Toolbox for digraph review.
Use blending board for /ch/, /th/, /sh/, and /wh/ review.
Use Elkonin boxes for students needing phoneme segmentation support.
Use vocabulary picture support for EL students.
reflectionPrompts: Did students understand cause and effect?
What should I remind Micayla before she teaches this lesson?
